1: In the second year after the exodus, in the first month, God commands Israel to keep the Passover at its appointed time. The repetition of Passover is covenant renewal. Christ reframes the Passover as the Lord's Supper, commanding his followers to repeat it 'in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19; 1 Cor 11:26).
2: The Israelites are to keep the Passover at its appointed time (môʿēd). Christ was crucified at Passover (John 18:28; 1 Cor 5:7), the appointed moment when the true Passover Lamb was slain 'according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God' (Acts 2:23).
3: The fourteenth of the first month, at twilight — with all its statutes and ordinances. The precise timing mirrors the precision of Christ's death: the Gospels note the very hours (Mark 15:25–34), the moment coinciding with the slaughter of the Passover lambs (John 19:14).
4: Moses tells the Israelites to keep the Passover, and they do so. Participation in the Lord's Supper is the community's required response to Christ's accomplished redemption — receiving and proclaiming his death until he comes (1 Cor 11:26).
5: They keep the Passover in the first month, fourteenth day, at twilight, in the wilderness — exactly as the LORD commanded. Even in the wilderness — the place of wandering — the Passover is kept. Christ commands his disciples to eat the Supper not only in triumph but in the wilderness of this present age, 'until he comes' (1 Cor 11:26).
6: Some men are unclean through contact with a corpse and cannot keep the Passover. Their question reveals a hunger to participate despite disqualification — anticipating all who come to Christ marked by death and defilement, longing for the true Passover feast (Matt 9:12–13).
7: The unclean men ask: why should we be kept from bringing the LORD's offering? Their persistence in seeking Passover participation despite legal disqualification pictures faith that longs to participate in God's redemption even when one's own condition seems to bar the way. Christ commended exactly this persistence (Matt 15:27–28).
8: Moses says he will wait to hear what the LORD will command. Moses as mediator brings the hard cases to God — the liminal, the excluded, those not covered by existing law. Christ as the greater mediator receives all such cases and always has a word of grace for those whom the law's letter seems to exclude (John 8:10–11).
9: God speaks to Moses: the principle of the second Passover is God's own compassionate provision. This divine initiative to include the excluded is characteristic of Christ's ministry — he reaches toward those outside normal covenant channels: Samaritans, Gentiles, the defiled, the unclean (Luke 17:16; Acts 10:15).
10: Anyone unclean from corpse-contact or on a distant journey may keep the Passover in the second month. The second Passover is God's accommodation to human limitation — a second chance built into the covenant structure. Christ himself is the second chance: 'God did not send his Son to condemn the world, but that the world through him might be saved' (John 3:17).
11: They shall keep it on the fourteenth of the second month at twilight — with unleavened bread and bitter herbs. The second Passover carries the same substance. Christ is the same yesterday, today, and forever (Heb 13:8); the Passover he provides for the excluded is no lesser feast, no diluted gospel.
12: 'Break no bone of it' — this specific ordinance is cited in John 19:36 when the soldiers bypass Jesus's legs at the cross, fulfilling this very regulation. Christ is the Passover lamb of whom no bone is broken (John 19:32–36; Ps 34:20).
13: The man who is clean and not traveling yet fails to keep the Passover shall be cut off — he has neglected the LORD's offering. Refusing the true Passover — rejecting Christ — likewise brings ultimate severance from the covenant community and its hope (Heb 10:29–31).
14: If a resident alien (gēr) wishes to keep the Passover, he must do so according to all its statutes — one law for native and stranger alike. The inclusion of the Gentile resident in the Passover covenant is the earliest marker of universal salvation; Paul sees this as the foundation for Gentile inclusion in Christ: 'neither Jew nor Greek' (Gal 3:28), one table and one Lamb for all (Eph 2:12–13).
15: The day the tabernacle was set up, the cloud covered it — by day the cloud, by night fire. The cloud by day and fire by night is the visible presence of YHWH with his people. The Spirit's fire-descent at Pentecost (Acts 2:3) continues the cloud-and-fire theophany in the new-covenant community.
16: The cloud covered the tabernacle perpetually — never absent, day or night. This mirrors Christ's promise: 'I am with you always, to the end of the age' (Matt 28:20). The perpetual cloud is the visual covenant of the never-departing God.
17: Whenever the cloud lifted from the tent, the Israelites would set out; wherever the cloud settled, they camped. Movement and rest entirely governed by God's initiative. The Christ-follower similarly moves and rests by the Spirit's guidance, not personal preference: 'As many as are led by the Spirit of God, they are the sons of God' (Rom 8:14).
18: At the LORD's command they set out; at the LORD's command they camped — as long as the cloud rested. The repeated 'at the LORD's command' establishes obedience to divine direction as the defining character of the wilderness community. Christ commands the same absolute responsiveness: 'Follow me' without reservation, wherever he leads (Matt 4:19).
19: When the cloud remained many days, the Israelites kept the LORD's service without setting out. Long encampments required sustained waiting — patience in apparent stillness. Christ calls his disciples to the same patient endurance: waiting in Jerusalem for the Spirit (Acts 1:4), waiting for his return (Rev 22:20).
20: When the cloud remained only a few days, they camped only a few days and then moved. Duration of rest is God's prerogative. The seasons of Christian life include both brief and extended periods of stability; the call is to receive each season from Christ's hand rather than dictating one's own schedule.
21: Sometimes the cloud remained only overnight; when it lifted at dawn they moved. Even overnight encampments at God's command. The disciple of Christ may experience only brief resting places before being called to move; faithfulness means holding all plans lightly and keeping pace with the Spirit.
22: Whether two days or a month or a year — however long the cloud remained — Israel stayed; when it lifted, they moved. The principle holds at every timescale. Christ's lordship over timing calls for trust regardless of how long the waiting stretches (Ps 27:14).
23: At the LORD's command they camped; at the LORD's command they set out — they kept the LORD's service according to his word through Moses. Christ is the Word of God incarnate (John 1:1), the living command whose voice governs the movement of his people into eternal life (John 10:27).
